input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based thermal incubator**, designed to regulate and maintain a target temperature within an insulated container. The system comprises the following key components:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box acting as a thermal barrier to minimize heat loss.
  - **Heatbed**: A heating element responsible for raising the internal temperature of the container.
  - **Fan**: A mechanical component for distributing heat uniformly inside the box.
  - **Temperature Sensors**:
    - Two sensors monitor the **internal temperature** of the container.
    - One sensor monitors the **external temperature** (ambient conditions).
  - **Controller**: A **Raspberry Pi** embedded system managing real-time control logic, communication, and data acquisition.
  The operational domain involves **thermal regulation** in a closed-loop system, where the goal is to achieve and sustain a predefined temperature regardless of internal content or external environmental fluctuations. The system is representative of CPSs due to its integration of **physical processes (thermal dynamics)** with **cyber components (control algorithms, sensors, and communication)**. Its suitability for **Digital Twin (DT) engineering** stems from the need for **real-time monitoring, predictive modeling, and adaptive control** to optimize performance, ensure safety, and reduce maintenance efforts. The open-source hardware and software specifications (available at [GitHub repository](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) provide a reproducible platform for DT development and validation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **specific services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  1. **Real-Time Monitoring**:
     - Continuously collects **time-series sensor data** (internal/external temperatures) from the PT via embedded sensors.
     - Uses **calibration algorithms** to update **thermal models** (e.g., differential equation-based models with 2 or 4 free parameters) to reflect the PT’s current state.
  2. **Predictive Simulation**:
     - Executes **dynamic simulations** (e.g., using differential equations) to predict the PT’s behavior under current or hypothetical operating conditions.
     - Supports **two model variants**:
       - A **simpler model** with **2 free parameters** (e.g., thermal conductivity, heat capacity).
       - A **more complex model** with **4 free parameters**, better capturing **thermal inertia** of the heatbed for improved accuracy.
     - Enables **what-if analysis** to assess consequences of changes (e.g., environmental disturbances, control adjustments).
  3. **Fault Detection and Safety Assurance**:
     - Implements **monitoring algorithms** to detect deviations from optimal/safe operation (e.g., temperature drifts, sensor failures).
     - Triggers **alerts** or **automated corrective actions** (e.g., adjusting heatbed power, activating backup cooling).
  4. **Optimization and Control**:
     - Uses **supervisory controllers** and **state estimators** to optimize temperature regulation.
     - Supports **reconfigurability** to adapt to changing environmental conditions (e.g., ambient temperature fluctuations).
  5. **Advanced Visualization**:
     - Provides **interactive visualizations** of the PT’s state, model predictions, and system performance metrics (e.g., temperature profiles, control signals).
  6. **Reduced Maintenance**:
     - Predicts **wear-and-tear** (e.g., heatbed degradation) and schedules preventive maintenance based on model-driven insights.
  **Target Users**:
     - **Engineers**: For system analysis, optimization, and debugging.
     - **Operators**: For real-time monitoring and decision support.
     - **Researchers**: For validating DT techniques (e.g., calibration methods, model@runtime approaches).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** for the incubator leverages the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, as explicitly or implicitly described in the documents:
  - **Hardware Platform**:
    - **Raspberry Pi**: Acts as the **embedded controller** for the physical system, interfacing with sensors (temperature probes) and actuators (heatbed, fan).
  - **Software and Communication**:
    - **RabbitMQ**: Mentioned as part of the **publicly available repository** ([INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) for **message brokering** between cyber and physical components, enabling real-time data exchange.
    - **Custom control logic**: Implemented on the Raspberry Pi, including a **bang-bang controller** (on-off control for the heatbed) as inferred from the incubator’s hardware setup.
  - **Modelling and Simulation**:
    - **Differential equation-based models**: Two variants developed for temperature prediction:
      - **2-parameter model**: Simpler representation of thermal dynamics.
      - **4-parameter model**: Enhanced accuracy for capturing **thermal inertia** of the heatbed, selected for further DT development.
    - **Calibration algorithms**: Used to **adjust model parameters** dynamically based on real-time sensor data, ensuring model fidelity.
  - **Data Management**:
    - **Sensor data logging**: Continuous collection of **temperature readings** from internal and external sensors, stored for model calibration and analysis.
  - **Development and Validation**:
    - **Open-source repository**: Hosts **hardware/software specifications**, **simulation models**, and **experimental results** ([INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)), serving as a **reference implementation** for DT techniques.
    - **Experimental validation**: Conducted to compare model performance (e.g., 2-parameter vs. 4-parameter models) and assess DT effectiveness in predicting thermal behavior.
  - **Not in Document**:
    - Specific **databases** (e.g., InfluxDB), **visualization tools** (e.g., Grafana), **advanced control algorithms** (e.g., PID, MPC), or **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing** are not mentioned in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system for the incubator case study relies on periodic data collection from the Physical Twin (PT) via two temperature sensors inside the styrofoam box and one outside, with data acquisition managed by a low-level driver that reads temperature values at unspecified but likely high-frequency intervals (e.g., seconds or sub-second intervals) to ensure real-time synchronization. The system employs a RabbitMQ server for synchronization and communication between the cyber (Raspberry Pi controller) and physical components, ensuring low-latency data exchange. The temporal granularity for monitoring and simulation is not explicitly quantified in the documents, but the use of a time-series database (implied for real-time visualization) and co-simulation for what-if scenarios suggests sub-second to second-level precision is required for accurate temperature modeling and controller optimization. Calibration algorithms are triggered on-demand to update models (e.g., Model B with four free parameters) based on observed deviations, though no specific synchronization frequency for calibration is provided. The system’s temporal dynamics are further constrained by the thermal inertia of the heatbed and the insulated container, which necessitates fine-grained time-scale modeling for predictive accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system for the incubator operates as a centralized architecture with a single instance managing both the cyber (Raspberry Pi-based controller) and physical components (styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and sensors). The system supports two distinct simulation models: **Model A** (with two free parameters) and **Model B** (with four free parameters), where Model B is preferred for its superior prediction of the heatbed’s thermal inertia. The hardware setup includes a 1-Wire bus for sensor communication, ensuring synchronized data collection from the two internal temperature sensors and one external sensor. No decentralized or federated Digital Twin instances are described; instead, the system relies on a cohesive framework where the Raspberry Pi orchestrates all cyber-physical interactions. The scope of responsibilities is divided between the physical plant (temperature regulation) and the cyber layer (model calibration, monitoring, and controller reconfiguration), but no hierarchical multiplicities or distributed coordination mechanisms are explicitly detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system for the incubator is structured as a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** with a clear separation between the **Physical Twin (PT)**—comprising the insulated styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and temperature sensors—and the **Cyber Twin (CT)**—implemented on a Raspberry Pi running a controller and simulation models. The architecture follows a **closed-loop orchestration** where data from the PT (collected via the 1-Wire bus and low-level driver) is relayed to the CT for processing. Key components include:
  - **Data Collection Layer**: Periodic sensor readings (temperature inside/outside the box) synchronized via RabbitMQ.
  - **Modeling Layer**: Two simulation models (A and B) with varying parameter counts, where Model B is dynamically calibrated to improve accuracy.
  - **Control Layer**: A supervisory controller (implemented on the Raspberry Pi) that adjusts the heatbed based on real-time or simulated data.
  - **Calibration Layer**: On-demand recalibration of Model B to adapt to changes in the PT’s behavior (e.g., thermal inertia variations).
  The system lacks explicit mention of distributed orchestration, microservices, or resource management frameworks, but the use of RabbitMQ suggests a message-passing paradigm for inter-component communication. The constellation does not include external dependencies beyond the PT’s environment (e.g., ambient temperature).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment. Two **behavioral models** were developed for the incubator case study: a **simplified model with two free parameters** and a **more complex model with four free parameters**, both predicting temperature dynamics inside the insulated container. The latter model demonstrated superior accuracy in capturing the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**, making it more suitable for DT applications. **Data** is collected from the **Physical Twin (PT)** via **sensors** (two internal temperature sensors and one external sensor) and processed through **calibration algorithms** to refine model parameters. The DT framework employs **models** (e.g., differential equations for dynamic behavior) and **algorithms** (e.g., fault detectors, state estimators, optimizers) to generate actionable insights. Data flows from sensors to models, which are updated via calibration, then inform **monitors** for safety and performance validation. The DT leverages **model relationships** where calibrated parameters (e.g., thermal properties) are dynamically adjusted to align with real-time sensor data, ensuring predictive accuracy under varying conditions. The **data management approach** involves real-time sensor fusion and model-driven updates, with a focus on **reproducibility** and **predictive fidelity** for the PT's behavior under current operational states.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The incubator's **physical acting components** include a **heatbed** (acting as the primary actuator) and a **fan** for thermal distribution. The **heatbed** regulates temperature via resistive heating, with actuation controlled by a **Raspberry Pi-based controller**. The system supports **remote control interfaces** through embedded software, enabling adjustments to heating power based on real-time feedback. **Actuation ranges** are constrained by the heatbed's thermal capacity and the fan's airflow limitations. **Safety constraints** are implicitly enforced via the DT's monitoring algorithms, which detect deviations from optimal performance (e.g., overheating or underheating) and trigger corrective actions. The **addressing mechanism** relies on the Raspberry Pi's communication layer, interfacing with the heatbed and fan via low-level control signals. No explicit **actuation precision specifications** are provided, but the DT's calibration algorithms ensure the system adapts to environmental changes (e.g., external temperature fluctuations) to maintain target conditions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises **three DS18S20 digital temperature sensors**: two positioned **internally** to measure air temperature within the insulated container and one **externally** to monitor ambient conditions. Sensors provide **temperature measurements with an accuracy of ±0.5°C** and are spatially distributed to ensure representative data collection. Data is acquired at unspecified sampling frequencies but is **averaged** to compute the **mean air temperature** inside the box, as described in the incubator case study. Transmission rates and protocols are not explicitly detailed, but the data is fed into the Digital Twin's calibration and monitoring algorithms for real-time analysis. No precision specifications beyond accuracy are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The DT prioritizes **model fidelity** through **calibration methods**, where the **four-parameter model** outperformed the two-parameter variant in capturing the **thermal inertia of the heatbed**, a key source of uncertainty. **Validation and verification** rely on experimental comparisons between simulated and real-world temperature profiles, ensuring the model's **predictive accuracy**. **Uncertainty sources** include environmental factors (e.g., external temperature variations) and sensor noise, quantified via model calibration residuals. **Error detection and correction** are handled by the DT's **monitoring algorithms**, which flag deviations and trigger recalibration or control adjustments. **Quality assurance** is embedded in the DT's closed-loop architecture, where real-time data continuously refines models to maintain alignment with the PT's behavior. The trade-off between **model accuracy** and **simulation speed** is acknowledged, as more detailed models (e.g., four-parameter) may require longer computational times but offer superior predictive performance.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the incubator Digital Twin (DT) system involves the transmission of **temperature sensor data** from the Physical Twin (PT) to the Digital Twin (DT) via a **RabbitMQ message broker**. The data flow includes the following technical details:

1. **Data Types Transmitted**:
   - **Temperature measurements**: Collected from **three sensors** (two inside the styrofoam box and one outside) via the **low-level driver**.
   - **Actuator states**: Status of the **heatbed** and **fan** (e.g., ON/OFF, duty cycles) transmitted as control feedback.

2. **Communication Protocol**:
   - **RabbitMQ** (open-source message broker) is used for **asynchronous message passing** between components. It supports **AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)** for reliable message delivery.

3. **Transmission Frequencies**:
   - The **low-level driver periodically reads temperature data** (exact frequency not specified but implied to be **real-time or near-real-time** for control purposes).
   - Sensor data is **uploaded to the RabbitMQ server** for consumption by the DT components.

4. **Event Triggers**:
   - Data transmission is **triggered by sensor readings** (e.g., temperature changes) and **controller commands** (e.g., heatbed/fan state updates).
   - The **controller listens to RabbitMQ** for commands and shares **control states** (e.g., heating duration, waiting duration) back to the broker.

5. **Data Quality Assurance**:
   - Implicit **safety protocols** in the **low-level driver** ensure data integrity (e.g., validation of sensor readings before transmission).
   - **Calibration algorithms** (mentioned in Document 1) are used to **update models** with the best estimate of parameters, ensuring data accuracy for simulations.

6. **Preprocessing Steps**:
   - Raw sensor data is **filtered or validated** by the low-level driver before being published to RabbitMQ.
   - The **controller processes temperature measurements** (e.g., applying bang-bang logic) before generating commands.

7. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - The DT **listens to messages** exchanged between the **controller and low-level driver** (as shown in **Figure 7**), enabling real-time synchronization.
   - **Models** (e.g., thermal dynamics models with 2 or 4 free parameters) are updated using **calibration algorithms** to align with PT behavior.

**Note**: Exact transmission frequencies (e.g., Hz) or message formats (e.g., JSON/Protobuf) are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT system is mediated through the **RabbitMQ server**, which transmits control commands from the DT (or the controller) to the **low-level driver**. The controller implements a **bang-bang-like control strategy** with parameters **LL (Lower Limit)**, **UL (Upper Limit)**, **H (Heating duration)**, and **C (Waiting duration)** to regulate the heatbed and fan. Commands are issued based on temperature measurements, with the controller turning the heatbed on until the temperature reaches the reference value, followed by a waiting period to avoid overshoot. The **low-level driver** enforces a **safe protocol** to ensure the PT remains in a secure state during actuation. No explicit details on message formats (e.g., JSON), authentication mechanisms, or conflict resolution are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical connection** for the incubator DT relies on a **RabbitMQ message broker** deployed on the same infrastructure as the controller (Raspberry Pi). The network architecture is centralized, with the RabbitMQ server acting as a communication hub between the **low-level driver**, **controller**, and DT components. The documents do not specify protocols (e.g., AMQP, TCP/IP), network topology (e.g., star, bus), or reliability mechanisms (e.g., redundancy, high availability). RabbitMQ is described as lightweight and easy to deploy, but no details on bandwidth, latency, or security protocols (e.g., encryption) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **hosting and deployment** of the incubator DT system is primarily **on-premises**, with the **Raspberry Pi** hosting the **controller**, **low-level driver**, and **RabbitMQ server**. The system is designed for simplicity, with no explicit mention of cloud deployment, edge computing, or scalability mechanisms. The DT leverages **simplified simulation models** (with 2 or 4 free parameters) for temperature prediction, and the infrastructure supports real-time communication between the PT and DT. No details on computational/storage requirements, containerization (e.g., Docker), or high-availability clustering are provided.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle for the incubator case study includes the following phases: **(1) Development Phase**: Creation of abstract representations (models) of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment, including **two simulation models** (one with **two free parameters** and another with **four free parameters**) to predict temperature dynamics. **(2) Integration Phase**: Models are integrated into the DT framework alongside **real-time data** from sensors (e.g., **two internal temperature sensors** and **one external temperature sensor**) and **calibration algorithms** to refine model parameters. **(3) Deployment Phase**: The DT is deployed to monitor and control the Physical Twin (PT) via a **Raspberry Pi controller**, enabling real-time adjustments. **(4) Operation Phase**: Continuous **monitoring** (via monitors checking for safe/optimal performance) and **adaptive decision-making** (human or algorithmic interventions when violations occur). **(5) Evolution Phase**: Iterative refinement of models and algorithms based on experimental results (e.g., the **four-parameter model** was selected for superior thermal inertia prediction). **Transition management** involves **calibration algorithms** updating models with real-time data, ensuring alignment between the DT and PT. **Lifecycle governance** is implied through structured experimentation (e.g., Section 4’s results validation) and open-source documentation for reproducibility (e.g., [GitHub repository](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process for the incubator DT follows a **model-driven, iterative engineering methodology** with the following technical details: **(1) Modeling**: Two **simulation models** (differential equation-based) were developed with varying abstraction levels (2 vs. 4 free parameters). The **four-parameter model** was empirically validated as superior for thermal inertia prediction. **(2) Data Integration**: Real-time **sensor data** (temperature readings from **three sensors**: two internal, one external) is ingested via the **Raspberry Pi controller** and fed into the DT framework. **(3) Calibration**: **Calibration algorithms** dynamically adjust model parameters to minimize discrepancies between the DT’s predictions and the PT’s observed behavior. **(4) Quality Assurance**: Validation is performed through **experimental results** (Section 4), comparing model predictions against real-world PT performance. **(5) Version Control**: Implicit in the open-source repository ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)), which tracks hardware/software specifications and model iterations. **(6) Evolution Management**: The DT evolves via **feedback loops** (e.g., monitor-triggered alerts) and **recalibration**, with future work (Section 5) suggesting further refinement of models and algorithms. **Technologies used**: Python (likely for model/simulation), Raspberry Pi (control/communication), and open-source tools for reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator DT provides **decision support** through the following capabilities: **(1) Insights Generation**: Real-time **temperature predictions** (via calibrated models) and **anomaly detection** (e.g., deviations from optimal thermal behavior). **(2) Analytics**: **Comparative analysis** of model accuracy (e.g., the **four-parameter model** outperformed the two-parameter model in thermal inertia prediction). **(3) Decision-Making Processes**: **Supervisory control** via the Raspberry Pi, which adjusts heating/fan operations based on DT-generated insights. **(4) User Interaction**: Human-in-the-loop decisions occur when **monitors detect violations** (e.g., unsafe temperatures), triggering alerts for manual intervention. **(5) Confidence Measures**: Model confidence is quantified through **experimental validation** (Section 4), where predictions are cross-checked against PT data. **Visualization**: Implied via advanced visualizations (Document 2) to aid interpretation of DT outputs (e.g., temperature trends, model discrepancies).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator incorporates **safety monitoring** through algorithms that check whether the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is performing **safely and optimally** under its current operating conditions. This is achieved via **monitoring components** that detect violations or deviations from expected behavior, triggering corrective actions (e.g., adjustments to the physical twin or alerts to human operators). The system leverages **calibration algorithms** to update models of the CPS and environment, ensuring accurate parameter estimation for reliable safety assessments. While the documents do not explicitly mention **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication, or access control mechanisms), the **Raspberry Pi controller** manages low-level operations such as actuating the heatbed, fan, and reading temperature sensors, implying a **fail-safe mechanism** for hardware control. The DT’s reliance on **real-time sensor data** (two internal temperature sensors and one external sensor) and **model-based predictions** (e.g., differential equations for thermal dynamics) further supports **safety-critical system considerations**, though no explicit threat mitigation strategies or compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 26262) are referenced.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.

2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

|Purple line is the
average room
temperature.|Col2|Col3| |---|---|---| ||Orange is Model B|Orange is Model B| Figure 13: The calibration result. approximation of the system magnitude and time scale, whereas the model B is better at predicting the inertia of the heatbed itself. 15 ### **5 Digital Twinning Goals** In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator case study. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

However, it includes significant unsupported speculation and details not found in the text, such as the reading frequency being 'likely high-frequency intervals (e.g., seconds or sub-second intervals)' and the use of a 'time-series database (implied for real-time visualization)'. The source text does not quantify the temporal granularity or mention a time-series database.

The extraction accurately describes the centralized architecture and the specific simulation models. It correctly states that there are 'two simulation models: Model A (with two free parameters) and Model B (with four free parameters)' and that Model B is preferred for predicting thermal inertia. It also correctly identifies the hardware setup details regarding the 1-Wire bus and sensor count.

The description accurately captures the Cyber-Physical System structure, separating the Physical Twin (styrofoam box, heatbed) and Cyber Twin (Raspberry Pi). It correctly identifies the layers (Data Collection, Modeling, Control) and the role of 'RabbitMQ server... for synchronisation and communication' between the controller and driver.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes the internal software setup and communication protocols (e.g., RabbitMQ, 1-Wire bus) within the incubator system but does not discuss integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for external data exchange, or integration with other Digital Twins.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the models (two and four parameters), the data sources (sensors), and the flow of data (sensors to calibration to monitors). However, it includes the term 'real-time sensor fusion', which is not present in the text; the source describes reading data and taking averages, not sensor fusion techniques. Otherwise, it is strongly grounded in the provided introduction and modeling sections. Supported by quotes: 'We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters', 'Data – collected from the PT through sensors over time', 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models... The resulting data is then used to inform monitors'.

Accurately describes the heatbed and fan as actuators and the Raspberry Pi as the controller. It correctly notes the communication setup via RabbitMQ. However, it infers 'remote control interfaces' where the text only specifies listening for commands, and it attributes safety enforcement primarily to DT monitoring algorithms, whereas the text also places a 'safe protocol' in the low-level driver. Supported by quotes: 'A heatbed to heat up the content... A fan to distribute the heating', 'Raspberry Pi 4 Model B was selected to implement the controller', 'safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver'.

The characteristic is highly accurate and well-grounded in the source text. It correctly specifies the sensor model (DS18S20), quantity (three), placement (two internal, one external), and accuracy (±0.5°C). It accurately notes that the data is averaged to find the mean temperature and correctly identifies that specific sampling frequencies or transmission protocols are not provided in the document.

The extraction is highly specific and detailed, correctly identifying the trade-off between model accuracy and speed (referencing the footnote), the superiority of the four-parameter model regarding thermal inertia, and the validation methods involving calibration and experimental comparison. It accurately captures the role of monitoring algorithms in detecting deviations. Supported by quotes: 'Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the thermal inertia of the heatbed itself', 'models can be described at different levels of abstraction... accurate models also are very slow', 'monitors that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally'.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.


4 [https://www.rabbitmq.com/](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)


7


Figure 6: Schematic of the software deployment of the components.


The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:


8


|Physical Twin|Digital Twin|
|---|---|
|||



Figure 7: Overview of communication in the Incubator. The arrows represent the information flow.


**LL** – Lower limit for temperature;
**UL** – Upper limit for temperature;
**H** – Heating duration;
**C** – Waiting duration.















Figure 8: Controller Statechart.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

The calibration process is accurately described (Sec 3.3: "non-linear least squares solver", "python Scipy package"). It correctly identifies the comparison of model accuracy (Abstract: "latter model was better"). A review of the source text reveals that while it mentions specific technologies (RabbitMQ, 1-Wire bus, Python) and references a GitHub repository, it does not mention any industry standards, compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO), certification requirements, or interoperability standards relevant to the Digital Twin definition. Therefore, the assessment that this information is missing from the document is correct.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator
